summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the rapid defeat of what he considered 'unbreakable' CAPTCHA implementations from Google, Hotmail, and Yahoo in early 2008. Despite viewing CAPTCHAs as a necessary evil, he acknowledges that profit-motivated attackers have rendered them largely ineffective, with even a 20% success rate being devastating when applied at scale. He argues that diversification of CAPTCHA techniques may be more effective than strengthening individual implementations, since attackers tailor their tools to specific targets. The post explores alternative approaches like image recognition and trivia questions, while warning that CAPTCHAs risk becoming intelligence tests that alienate legitimate users.
key_points: All three 'unbreakable' CAPTCHAs (Google, Hotmail, Yahoo) were defeated within months, demonstrating how quickly security measures can fall
A 20% success rate is devastating because spammers use botnets to send thousands of simultaneous requests
CAPTCHAs still serve as 'speed bumps' against unsophisticated attacks, even if they can't stop determined commercial attackers
Diversification of CAPTCHA techniques may be more effective than strengthening any single implementation
There's an inherent tension between making CAPTCHAs strong enough to stop bots and keeping them accessible to legitimate users
money_quotes: I am no fan of CAPTCHA. I view them as a necessary and important evil, one of precious few things separating average internet users from a torrential deluge of email, comment, and forum spam.
A twenty percent success rate doesn't sound like much, but these spammers are harnessing networks of compromised PCs to send out thousands upon thousands of simultaneous sign-up requests.
With twenty percent success rate you might as well put a fork in that thing – it's done.
There's simply too much money to be made in email spam for the commercial CAPTCHA algorithms, regardless of how good they may be, to survive forever.
Perhaps proliferation and evolution of many different CAPTCHA techniques is the most effective prevention.
At some point, unfortunately, CAPTCHA devolves from a simple human reading test into an intelligence test or an acuity test.
I don't have the all answers, but one thing is for sure: I hate spammers.
key_insight: The economics of spam mean that even the best CAPTCHAs will eventually be broken, making diversification and evolution more important than any single 'unbreakable' solution.
